title: 万圣节前夜的谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\126-万圣节前夜的谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Poirot discusses the death of Mrs. Llewellyn-Smith, a wealthy widow of a shipbuilder who lived in Stone Quarry House, a Victorian mansion featuring an underground garden created from an abandoned quarry. While her death was attributed to natural causes—specifically heart failure following exertion in her garden—Poirot investigates potential foul play. 

The discussion reveals a controversy regarding a codicil to her will. While her previous wills left most property to her nephew and his wife, the Drakes, this new codicil left everything to a young nurse from Central Europe. The Manchester law firm Fuller, Harrison, and Leedbetter identified the codicil as a forgery. It was noted that the nurse had experience mimicking handwriting, having previously written letters for Lady Lintin-Smith. Following extensive questioning by the lawyers, the nurse fled the country, leaving her belongings behind. There is a suggestion that the nurse may have served the victim a drink that tasted strange or bitter before her death.

Poirot also inquires about Charlotte Belfield, a sixteen-year-old shop assistant found dead with multiple head injuries near a mining area. Two young men, Peter Gordon and Thomas Heddle, were identified as potential suspects. Peter Gordon is an unemployed man with a history of petty theft and associations with delinquents. Thomas Heddle is a shy, anxious stationery store worker with a stammer. Although jealousy was considered a possible motive, both men had alibis: Heddle’s mother claimed he was home with her, and Gordon’s friends provided an alibi, though the credibility of these statements was questioned.
